data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Technological obsolescence of bronze procurement networks rendered palace systems economically insolvent when iron metallurgy democratized military production, while Egypt's Nubian gold and copper monopoly sustained transitional extraction", "mechanism": "Palace economies were structurally dependent on bronze-circulation prestige goods and chariot-based military systems requiring controlled tin-copper supply chains. Widespread iron adoption (documented c. 1200 BCE in Cyprus and Levant) collapsed bronze premium values, eliminating the fiscal base for redistribution. Egypt's Upper Egyptian/Nubian mining concessions and gold-based international credit functioned as hard-currency reserve during monetary transition.", "scope": {"covers": ["Palace economy collapse", "Military transformation", "Egyptian fiscal continuity"], "excludes": ["Climate events", "Sea Peoples", "Ideological factors", "Earthquake damage"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence that iron weapons replaced bronze in palace armories before rather than after collapse", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Continued operation of Cypriot copper smelting and tin trade routes through 1100 BCE", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian textual evidence of bronze (not gold) remaining dominant in Ramesside military and ritual contexts", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Mycenaean destruction layers showing iron weapons in burn layers or iron dominance in post-palatial assemblages", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Challenges technological determinism critiques; iron adoption typically dated post-collapse (c. 1050-1000 BCE in Greece), making this mechanism temporally suspect. Risks conflating symptom and cause."}
